title: Who Loses Control First?
subtitle: Threshold Competition in the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict

alternate_identifiers.description: Short citation shown or implied by the EPINOVA working paper format

description: This working paper analyzes the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict as a form of threshold competition rather than a conventional contest for decisive military victory. It introduces the loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) as an analytical lens for assessing how cumulative military, fiscal, political, economic, informational, and legitimacy pressures can push actors beyond their capacity to regulate escalation. The paper compares three actor-specific pathways: U.S. fiscal–strategic overextension, Israeli escalation lock-in, and Iran's legitimacy–retaliation loop.
abstract: This paper analyzes the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict as a form of threshold competition rather than a contest for decisive military victory. It introduces the concept of the loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) to explain how actors lose the ability to regulate escalation under cumulative systemic pressure. The analysis identifies three distinct pathways: U.S. fiscal–strategic overextension, Israeli escalation lock-in, and Iran’s legitimacy–retaliation loop. The paper argues that the key variable in contemporary conflict is not battlefield superiority, but the ability to delay crossing actor-specific thresholds of uncontrollability.

series_information.series_description: The WP-F series explores AI governance and strategic risk through comparative and historical diagnostics, examining how inherited strategic and regulatory logics break down when transferred into AI-mediated contexts.

methodology.method_type: Conceptual and diagnostic working paper using comparative actor-pathway analysis and synthesis of open-source reporting and prior EPINOVA systemic warfare research.
methodology.analytical_focus: The paper reframes the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict as threshold competition under cumulative systemic pressure, asking which actor loses the capacity to regulate escalation first rather than which actor achieves decisive victory.

core_concepts.name: Loss-of-Control Threshold
core_concepts.description: The point at which cumulative pressures across military, political, economic, and informational systems exceed an actor's capacity to regulate escalation dynamics.

core_concepts.name: Threshold competition
core_concepts.description: A conflict condition in which actors compete to delay crossing actor-specific thresholds of uncontrollability rather than to secure decisive battlefield victory.

core_concepts.name: Systemic pressure
core_concepts.description: Nonlinear pressure generated by the interaction of node criticality, cascading effects, temporal persistence, and information amplification.

actor_pathways.outcome_logic: Sustaining global commitments becomes increasingly difficult under fiscal pressure, cross-regional strategic strain, domestic political constraints, and cost-imposition asymmetry.
actor_pathways.threshold_pathway: Constrained strategic choices between strategic contraction and high-risk escalation.

actor_pathways.outcome_logic: Escalation generates deterrence, deterrence requires continued escalation, and continued escalation reduces flexibility and increases risk.
actor_pathways.threshold_pathway: De-escalation weakens deterrence, while continued escalation reduces control.

actor_pathways.outcome_logic: Failure to retaliate weakens domestic and regional credibility, while retaliation increases exposure to further escalation.
actor_pathways.threshold_pathway: Maintaining deterrence requires continued retaliation, while each response increases the probability of more severe counterstrikes.

relationship_to_prior_frameworks.framework: Systemic Warfare in the Networked Age
relationship_to_prior_frameworks.relationship: The LoCT framework extends systemic warfare logic by identifying actor-specific tipping points under cumulative pressure.
The paper uses the vulnerability of globally deployed military networks and cost-imposition dynamics as a basis for analyzing U.S. overextension risk.
LoCT is conceptually aligned with SPI-style nonlinear accumulation of systemic stress.

dimensions.name: U.S. Fiscal–Strategic Overextension
dimensions.description: Risk pathway in which fiscal burden, global commitments, cross-regional force allocation, domestic legitimacy, and cost-imposition asymmetry narrow U.S. strategic options.

dimensions.name: Israeli Escalation Lock-In
dimensions.description: Risk pathway in which high-intensity operations raise the baseline of escalation and make de-escalation increasingly costly.

dimensions.name: Iranian Legitimacy–Retaliation Loop
dimensions.description: Risk pathway in which Iran faces pressure to retaliate to preserve credibility while each retaliation increases exposure to counter-escalation.

dimensions.name: Systemic Pressure
dimensions.description: Cumulative pressure across military, political, economic, and informational systems that can produce nonlinear loss-of-control effects.

dimensions.name: Threshold Competition
dimensions.description: Strategic interaction in which the decisive variable is each actor's capacity to delay crossing thresholds of uncontrollability.

key_findings: The 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict is better understood as threshold competition than as a conventional contest for decisive military victory.
The loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) captures the point at which cumulative systemic pressure exceeds an actor's capacity to regulate escalation dynamics.
The United States faces a pathway of fiscal–strategic overextension driven by war funding burdens, cross-regional strategic strain, domestic political constraints, and cost-imposition asymmetry.
Israel faces escalation lock-in, where deterrence depends on continued escalation but continued escalation reduces maneuver space and control.
Iran faces a legitimacy–retaliation loop, where failure to respond damages credibility while retaliation increases exposure to further escalation.

The decisive variable in contemporary systemic warfare is not battlefield superiority alone, but the capacity to absorb and manage cumulative pressure without crossing actor-specific thresholds of uncontrollability.
The actor that loses control first is the actor that loses, even absent a conventional battlefield defeat.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and early ORCID-derived metadata. The working paper presents diagnostic and exploratory analysis intended to examine inherited strategic and governance logics under AI-mediated conditions. It does not constitute policy recommendations, predictive assessments, or official positions of any institution.
